questions.text: I tend to focus on the problem being discussed rather than on how people are feeling about it.
I notice that I don't get swept up in the emotional energy of crowds or groups.
I notice that my emotional activation comes with distinct physical markers every time.
I hold the view that composure during emotionally charged situations is what allows me to be a stabilizing presence for others.
I detect when a group's energy shifts from casual to serious.
I prefer to let my day unfold naturally rather than follow a rigid schedule.
I feel that my personal ambitions deserve dedicated time and resources.
I feel energized when people follow my lead.
I hold the view that the moral claims generated by suffering are among the most legitimate demands that can be placed on a person.
I instinctively adapt to whatever structure the group has established.
I believe that consistent small efforts compound into remarkable results over time.
I feel that the consistency of my moral attention to suffering is the most reliable evidence I have for my own character.
I find that I naturally read the room before deciding how to contribute to a conversation.
I hold the view that groups need a strong voice to stay focused.
I am the type of person who always has a five-year plan or long-range vision.
I find that I am happiest when I have nothing left because I gave it all away.
I am prone to sudden surges of emotion that catch me off guard.
I would delay my own education to support a family member through theirs.
